title: The shortest trustworthy route into AI agents and MCP
dek: Start with the mental model, then trace how tools, context, and permissions turn a model into a useful agent.

orientation.title: In one sentence
orientation.body: An AI agent is a model inside a loop that can observe context, choose an action, call a tool, inspect the result, and continue toward a goal. MCP is a standard way for an AI application to discover and use external tools and resources without every integration becoming a one-off adapter.

path.label: Get the shape
path.title: Model, loop, tools
path.body: Learn the difference between a chat completion, a workflow, and an agent. The useful shift is not that the model becomes autonomous. It is that the surrounding system gives it a bounded loop and observable actions.

path.label: See the contract
path.title: MCP as a shared language
path.body: MCP separates the host application from servers that expose tools, resources, and prompts. That separation makes integrations portable, but it also makes capability discovery and permission boundaries first-class design problems.

path.label: Build judgment
path.title: Reliability is the product
path.body: Study approval gates, narrow tool schemas, trace logs, retries, and evals. A polished agent is less about adding more autonomy and more about making every consequential action explainable and reversible.

claims.text: An agent is a bounded control loop around a model, not a model with a personality.
claims.explanation: The primary sources describe agents in terms of planning, tool use, state, and loop control rather than an independent form of agency.

claims.text: MCP improves integration portability by standardizing how hosts discover tools and resources.
claims.explanation: The protocol specification and introduction agree on the host, client, and server relationship.

claims.text: More tool access does not automatically make an agent more useful.
claims.explanation: The engineering guidance converges on narrow schemas, explicit approvals, and observability as the conditions that make tool use reliable.

claims.text: The best architecture depends on whether the task is a deterministic workflow or an open-ended decision loop.
claims.explanation: Agent frameworks emphasize flexible loops, while production guidance recommends keeping predictable work deterministic. The disagreement is about task shape, not a single universal winner.

sources.title: Model Context Protocol specification
sources.reason: The protocol’s source of truth for lifecycle, tools, resources, and transport.

sources.title: Introducing the Model Context Protocol
sources.reason: Explains the integration problem MCP was designed to solve.

sources.title: Agents SDK documentation
sources.reason: Grounds the agent loop in handoffs, tools, guardrails, and traces.

sources.title: Building effective agents
sources.reason: A useful distinction between workflows and agents, with patterns for choosing less autonomy.

sources.title: Agentic AI security guidance
sources.reason: Adds the missing production lens: permission boundaries, tool abuse, and observability.

next: Build a read-only MCP server and inspect the tool schema it exposes.
Add an approval gate before a tool can change external state.
Trace one complete run from user request to tool result and evaluate every step.
